does: This paper introduces PAL, a framework that models human preferences in alignment of large foundation models by incorporating the plurality of diverse human opinions rather than assuming universal preferences. The method uses an ideal point model as a lens to reformulate preference learning, employing mixture modeling to simultaneously capture multiple preference distributions while learning a shared latent space across different preferences. The approach departs from standard Bradley-Terry-Luce reward model assumptions by allowing heterogeneous preferences to coexist, enabling the framework to adapt pairwise comparisons from multiple annotators with different underlying preference structures into a unified representation that respects individual variation.
connects: This work responds to limitations in existing alignment approaches that rely on collecting pairwise human comparisons and learning reward models through the Bradley-Terry-Luce framework, a standard assumption in preference-based reinforcement learning and RLHF methods. The paper builds on foundational work in preference modeling and human feedback incorporation into large models, extending lines of research in human-AI alignment that have typically abstracted away individual differences. It connects to broader literature on mixture models and latent space learning, as well as ideal point models from voting theory and preference aggregation, bringing methodologies from social choice theory to bear on the practical problem of foundation model alignment.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more nuanced alignment procedures that respect human value pluralism rather than forcing consensus on monolithic preference structures. Future work could develop methods for efficiently discovering and modeling human preference clusters, enabling personalized model behavior that adapts to different user groups or contexts. The shared latent space representation could support few-shot preference adaptation and transfer learning across related domains. Additionally, this approach could inform better data collection strategies for alignment that explicitly account for preference diversity, potentially reducing annotation requirements while improving model robustness to preference disagreement and enabling more democratic approaches to foundation model deployment.
